symptoms.symptom_name: Progression from 6 months of exertional foot claudication to 48 hours of unremitting rest pain
symptoms.onset: claudication onset approximately 6 months ago; rest pain onset 48 hours ago
relieving_factors: hanging the foot over the edge of the bed

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Progression from exertional claudication to rest pain over a short period in a young heavy smoker is a classic pattern of critical limb ischaemia from a small-vessel occlusive process.
Claudication progressing to rest pain is also a recognised pattern of severe atherosclerotic PAD, though less typical at this young age without other atherosclerotic risk factors.

symptoms.symptom_name: Heavy current tobacco use — approximately 30 cigarettes/day for 15 years

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Heavy, sustained tobacco/nicotine exposure is considered essential to the pathogenesis of thromboangiitis obliterans; the diagnosis is very rarely made in the absence of significant tobacco or nicotine use, making this a near-defining feature in combination with the clinical pattern.
Smoking is also a major risk factor for atherosclerotic PAD, so this feature alone does not fully distinguish between the two, but the magnitude and patient age favour thromboangiitis obliterans.

symptoms.symptom_name: Second toe on the right foot has turned completely black and cold over the preceding day (digital gangrene)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Distal digital gangrene in a young heavy smoker, with a background of claudication and preserved proximal circulation, is a hallmark presentation of thromboangiitis obliterans causing critical limb ischaemia.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Any cause of critical limb ischaemia
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Visible tissue necrosis is objective, definitive evidence of critical limb ischaemia regardless of underlying aetiology and mandates emergency vascular assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: History of intermittent tender, red, cord-like lesions on the lower legs (suggestive of migratory superficial thrombophlebitis)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Recurrent migratory superficial thrombophlebitis is a well-recognised associated feature of thromboangiitis obliterans, reflecting the same underlying inflammatory thrombotic process affecting both arteries and superficial veins.
Migratory superficial thrombophlebitis is not a typical feature of atherosclerotic PAD, making this diagnosis relatively less likely when this history is present.

symptoms.symptom_name: No personal or family history of diabetes

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of diabetes substantially reduces the probability of diabetic microvascular/neuropathic foot disease as the primary cause of the presentation.
Absence of diabetes does not itself support thromboangiitis obliterans but helps exclude a competing diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Palpable femoral and popliteal pulses bilaterally with absent dorsalis pedis/posterior tibial pulses on the right and severely diminished on the left; capillary refill >5 seconds in the right foot

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The pattern of preserved proximal pulses with absent distal pulses is the classic clinical hallmark distinguishing thromboangiitis obliterans from proximal atherosclerotic disease, where more proximal pulses would typically also be reduced or absent.
Normal proximal (femoral/popliteal) pulses argue against significant proximal atherosclerotic occlusive disease as the primary cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Corkscrew-appearing collateral vessels on peripheral angiography

investigation_details.expected_result: Corkscrew-shaped collateral vessels around areas of segmental distal arterial occlusion, sparing proximal vessels — classic radiological sign of thromboangiitis obliterans
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Diagnosis and extent of vascular occlusion not confirmed; vascular surgical planning for possible revascularisation or amputation level cannot proceed without this imaging.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Corkscrew collaterals on angiography are a classic and highly characteristic radiological finding for thromboangiitis obliterans.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of a young patient with heavy, longstanding tobacco use, progressive claudication evolving into unremitting rest pain, visible digital gangrene, and a history of migratory superficial thrombophlebitis together constitutes a classic constellation for thromboangiitis obliterans causing critical limb ischaemia — a limb-threatening emergency that must be recognised and escalated regardless of definitive diagnostic confirmation, which requires clinician examination and imaging.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Peripheral vascular examination (pulse mapping — femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) and capillary refill assessment
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Essential first-line clinical assessment to characterise the pattern and severity of arterial insufficiency and to distinguish proximal atherosclerotic disease from distal small-vessel occlusive disease. Cannot be performed via telehealth — requires emergency department or urgent in-person vascular assessment.
Required to confirm the diagnosis (corkscrew collaterals), map the extent of disease, and guide vascular surgical decision-making regarding possible revascularisation versus amputation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count, inflammatory markers, coagulation studies, and thrombophilia/autoimmune screen
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Useful to exclude alternative or contributing causes of a hypercoagulable or vasculitic state, and to assess for signs of secondary infection given the area of gangrene.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Elective outpatient duplex ultrasound for chronic claudication work-up
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not appropriate as the primary next step given this is now a limb-threatening emergency with tissue necrosis — urgent in-person vascular assessment and imaging take priority over routine outpatient investigation pathways.
